title: Chocolate and hazelnut cake
subtitle: A nearly flourless cake with dark chocolate and ground hazelnuts
excerpt: A dense, fudgy cake that is almost flourless: the structure comes from eggs whisked with sugar and dark chocolate, with ground hazelnuts replacing most of the flour. The result is rich without being heavy, and has a slightly sunken centre by design.
sourceNotes: Flourless and nearly flourless chocolate cakes using ground nuts in place of flour are well established in European baking, with documented versions in Austrian and Italian traditions from the nineteenth century onward. The technique here follows the egg-foam-and-melted-chocolate method common across these traditions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze whole or in slices. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps at room temperature for 3 days, covered. The texture improves after resting overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C; the centre will appear slightly underdone at the skewer test; that is correct

glossaryTerms.definition: In flourless or nearly flourless cakes, the structure that holds the cake together comes from eggs rather than gluten. Whole eggs or separated eggs whisked with sugar trap air and, when baked, set around the melted chocolate and nut mass. The result is a denser, moister crumb than a flour-based cake. The centre of a flourless cake should still have a slight wobble when the tin comes out of the oven; it firms as it cools.

content.text: This cake is held together by eggs, not flour. The

gives the cake a fudgy, dense crumb that is quite different from a sponge. The hazelnuts are used ground; they add body and a faint nuttiness that sits underneath the chocolate without announcing itself separately. The centre will look underdone when you take it from the oven. It will firm as it cools. A sunken centre is the expected result, not a failure.
Prepare the tin and melt the chocolate

Preheat the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional). Grease the tin, line the base with baking paper, and dust the sides with cocoa powder. Melt the dark chocolate and butter together in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes.

Beat the egg yolks with 100 g of the caster sugar until thick and pale, about 3 minutes. Stir into the cooled chocolate mixture along with the ground hazelnuts and flour. In a clean bowl, whisk the egg whites with a pinch of salt until they hold soft peaks, then add the remaining 50 g of sugar and whisk until glossy. Fold one-third of the whites into the chocolate mixture to loosen it, then fold in the remainder in two additions, trying to preserve as much volume as possible.

Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 28 to 32 minutes. The edges will be set and pulling from the tin; the centre will still wobble slightly when you move the tin. A skewer inserted in the centre should come out with a few moist crumbs. Do not bake until the centre is dry. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes, then turn out carefully. The cake is fragile when warm. Leave on the rack for at least another 30 minutes before slicing. Serve dusted with cocoa powder.
